introHtml: Database questions assess your ability to choose and operate storage systems correctly. SQL query writing, index internals, the ACID properties, normalization, and NoSQL tradeoffs are core topics. Redis patterns, connection pooling, and the N+1 query problem come up often in practical interviews.

topics.bodyHtml:
ACID Properties

PropertyMeaningExample
AtomicityAll or nothingBank transfer: debit AND credit or neither
ConsistencyDB goes valid state to valid stateBalance can't go negative if constraint set
IsolationConcurrent txns don't interfereTwo transfers don't see each other's partial state
DurabilityCommitted data survives crashWritten to WAL before confirming

Transaction Isolation Levels

LevelDirty ReadNon-repeatable ReadPhantom Read
READ UNCOMMITTEDpossiblepossiblepossible
READ COMMITTED (PG default)preventedpossiblepossible
REPEATABLE READpreventedpreventedpossible
SERIALIZABLEpreventedpreventedprevented
BEGIN;
SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1;
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2;
COMMIT;

Indexes

-- B-tree (default): equality + range queries
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_date ON orders(user_id, created_at DESC);

-- Partial index: index subset of rows
CREATE INDEX idx_active_users ON users(email) WHERE active = true;

-- GIN: full-text search, jsonb, arrays
CREATE INDEX idx_posts_tsv ON posts USING GIN(to_tsvector('english', content));
CREATE INDEX idx_tags ON posts USING GIN(tags);

-- Covering index: include extra columns to avoid heap fetch
CREATE INDEX idx_orders ON orders(user_id) INCLUDE (total, status);

EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT) SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 1;

Window Functions

SELECT
  user_id, amount,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY created_at DESC) AS rn,
  RANK()       OVER (ORDER BY amount DESC),
  LAG(amount)  OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY created_at),
  LEAD(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY created_at),
  SUM(amount)  OVER (PARTITION BY user_id
                     ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total
FROM orders;

-- Latest order per user
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY created_at DESC) AS rn
  FROM orders
) r WHERE rn = 1;

CTEs and Recursive CTEs

WITH recent AS (
  SELECT * FROM orders WHERE created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
),
totals AS (
  SELECT user_id, SUM(amount) AS total FROM recent GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT u.name, t.total FROM users u JOIN totals t ON u.id = t.user_id;

-- Recursive CTE: org chart
WITH RECURSIVE tree AS (
  SELECT id, name, manager_id, 0 AS depth
  FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, t.depth + 1
  FROM employees e JOIN tree t ON e.manager_id = t.id
)
SELECT * FROM tree;

JOINs

-- INNER: matching rows only
SELECT u.name, o.total FROM users u INNER JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;
-- LEFT: all left rows + matched right (NULL if no match)
SELECT u.name, COUNT(o.id) FROM users u LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id GROUP BY u.id;
-- SELF JOIN: employee to manager
SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager
FROM employees e LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;

Normalization

1NF: atomic values, no repeating groups.
2NF: 1NF + no partial dependencies on composite PK.
3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependencies (non-key cols depend only on PK).
Denormalization: trade write complexity for read performance.

JSONB, Partitioning, Replication

-- JSONB column
SELECT data->>'name' FROM products;
SELECT * FROM products WHERE data @> '{"category": "electronics"}';
CREATE INDEX ON products USING GIN(data);

-- Range partitioning
CREATE TABLE orders (order_date DATE) PARTITION BY RANGE (order_date);
CREATE TABLE orders_2024 PARTITION OF orders FOR VALUES FROM ('2024-01-01') TO ('2025-01-01');

-- pg_stat_statements: find slow queries
SELECT query, calls, mean_exec_time FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC LIMIT 10;

// CRUD
db.users.insertOne({ name: "Alice", age: 30, tags: ["admin"] });
db.users.findOne({ email: "a@b.com" });
db.users.find({ age: { $gte: 18 }, active: true }).sort({ name: 1 }).limit(10);
db.users.updateOne({ _id: id }, { $set: { name: "Bob" }, $push: { tags: "user" } });
db.users.deleteMany({ active: false });

// Aggregation pipeline
db.orders.aggregate([
  { $match: { status: "completed" } },
  { $group: { _id: "$userId", total: { $sum: "$amount" }, count: { $count: {} } } },
  { $sort: { total: -1 } },
  { $limit: 10 },
  { $lookup: { from: "users", localField: "_id", foreignField: "_id", as: "user" } },
  { $unwind: "$user" },
  { $project: { userName: "$user.name", total: 1 } }
]);

// Indexes
db.users.createIndex({ email: 1 }, { unique: true });
db.posts.createIndex({ title: "text", content: "text" });
db.orders.createIndex({ userId: 1, createdAt: -1 });

// explain
db.users.find({ email: "a@b.com" }).explain("executionStats");

// Schema design patterns:
// Embedded: fast reads, update anomalies if deeply nested
// Referenced: normalized, requires lookup/$lookup
// Bucket pattern: group time-series data
// Outlier: most-case embedded, overflow ref for large outliers

// Replica set: primary + secondaries, automatic failover
// Sharding: shard key determines data distribution
// Change streams: real-time DB change notifications

TypeExamplesUse CasesStrengthsWeaknesses
RelationalPostgreSQL, MySQLFinancial, ERP, most appsACID, complex queries, joinsHorizontal scale harder
DocumentMongoDB, CouchDBContent, catalogs, user profilesFlexible schema, nested dataJoins expensive
Key-ValueRedis, DynamoDBSessions, cache, leaderboardsExtremely fast, simpleLimited query ability
ColumnCassandra, HBaseTime series, write-heavyWrite performance, wide rowsNo joins, eventual consistency
GraphNeo4j, ArangoDBSocial networks, recommendationsRelationship traversalNiche, complex to operate
SearchElasticsearchFull-text search, logsPowerful text search, analyticsNot primary DB
Time-seriesInfluxDB, TimescaleDBMetrics, IoTOptimized for time queriesSingle use case

// Data structures
SET key "value" EX 3600    // String with TTL
INCR counter               // Atomic increment

LPUSH list a b c           // List (push left)
LRANGE list 0 -1           // Get all

HSET user:1 name "Alice" age 30   // Hash
HGETALL user:1

SADD tags "js" "node"      // Set
SMEMBERS tags

// Sorted Set: leaderboard
ZADD leaderboard 1500 "Alice"
ZADD leaderboard 2000 "Bob"
ZRANGE leaderboard 0 -1 WITHSCORES REV  // top scores
ZRANK leaderboard "Alice"

// Persistence: RDB (snapshot), AOF (log every write), both

// Distributed lock (Redlock)
const lock = await redis.set("lock:resource", uuid, "NX", "PX", 30000);
// NX: only if not exists; PX: millisecond TTL

// Rate limiting
const now = Date.now();
const pipe = redis.pipeline();
pipe.zremrangebyscore(key, 0, now - 60000);
pipe.zadd(key, now, `${now}-${Math.random()}`);
pipe.zcard(key);
pipe.pexpire(key, 60000);
const [[,],[,],[,count]] = await pipe.exec();
if (count > 100) throw new RateLimitError();

// Pub/Sub
publisher.publish("channel", JSON.stringify(event));
subscriber.subscribe("channel", (msg) => handle(JSON.parse(msg)));

// Redis Cluster: 16384 hash slots, hash_slot = CRC16(key) % 16384
// Hash tags for multi-key ops: {user}:data1, {user}:data2 go same slot

items.question: How would you explain the difference between an index scan and a full table scan?
items.answerHtml: A full table scan reads every row because the database has no cheaper access path. An index scan uses an auxiliary data structure to narrow down matching rows quickly, often trading extra write cost and storage for faster reads.
items.flashcardFront: Why do indexes speed up reads?
items.flashcardBackHtml: They provide a faster path to matching rows so the database does not need to scan the whole table.

items.question: When would you choose PostgreSQL over MongoDB, and when would the reverse make sense?
items.answerHtml: Choose PostgreSQL when strong relational modeling, joins, transactions, constraints, and mature SQL tooling matter. Choose MongoDB when document flexibility, rapidly evolving shapes, or denormalized read models fit the workload better. Good answers center on access patterns and consistency needs, not hype.
items.flashcardBackHtml: Postgres is stronger for relational consistency and joins; MongoDB is stronger for flexible document-oriented models.

items.question: What makes Redis different from a primary database, and when is that difference valuable?
items.answerHtml: Redis is an in-memory data structure store optimized for extremely low latency access patterns such as caching, counters, locks, queues, and ephemeral coordination. It can persist data, but it is usually used where speed and simple structures matter more than rich querying and durable relational guarantees.

items.question: A query is fast in development but slow in production with millions of rows. What would you inspect first?
items.answerHtml: Check the execution plan, existing indexes, cardinality estimates, join order, filter selectivity, and whether the query is forcing sort or scan-heavy work. I would also compare data distribution and parameter values because production slowness often comes from scale and skew, not just syntax.

items.question: What anomalies can happen when isolation is too weak, and how would you explain them quickly?
items.answerHtml: Common anomalies include dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantom reads. A strong short answer says weaker isolation improves concurrency but risks seeing data that changes unexpectedly during a transaction, while stronger isolation reduces anomalies at the cost of contention.

items.question: Your cache and database diverge after writes. What strategies reduce stale reads?
items.answerHtml: Use clear cache invalidation rules, shorter TTLs where needed, versioned keys, or write-through patterns when consistency matters. If the cache is auxiliary rather than authoritative, the application should also tolerate occasional misses and stale values instead of assuming perfect coherence.

keyTakeaways.title: ⚡ Key Takeaways
itemsHtml: ACID: Atomicity (all or nothing), Consistency (valid state), Isolation (concurrent txns don't interfere), Durability (committed data survives crashes)
Composite indexes are only used when the leftmost column is in the query — column order matters critically
N+1 query problem: fetching a list then querying each item individually; solve with JOIN, include, or DataLoader
EXPLAIN ANALYZE reveals actual execution costs; check for sequential scans on large tables
Redis sorted sets (ZADD, ZRANGE, ZRANK) are the canonical solution for leaderboards
MongoDB: embed data that is read together, reference data that is updated independently
Connection pooling is essential in production; pg.Pool / Mongoose pool prevent connection exhaustion
